chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue - FinTech Global
articles.snippet: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue  FinTech Global

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

articles.title: AdvanCell Strengthens Executive Leadership to Accelerate U.S. Expansion and Scale its Global Targeted Alpha Therapy Platform - Business Wire
articles.snippet: AdvanCell Strengthens Executive Leadership to Accelerate U.S. Expansion and Scale its Global Targeted Alpha Therapy Platform  Business Wire

post1.text:
Adding senior executives does not fix a leadership problem. It multiplies it.

When a company scales from $50M toward $500M, the instinct is to hire headcount at the top and assume the culture follows. It rarely does. Leadership behavior sets the operating system. New titles without a clear leadership framework just add noise.

Recently, Buffini & Company made headlines for bringing on three senior executives specifically to scale their coaching business. The move is notable not because of the hires themselves, but because of the intentionality behind them. Growth was the goal. Leadership architecture was the vehicle.

Here is what scaling leaders actually need to know:

1. Your leadership model must be built before you hire into it, not after.
2. Every senior addition either reinforces your culture or quietly erodes it.
3. The gap between $50M and $500M is almost never a strategy problem. It is a leadership alignment problem.

What is the one leadership behavior at your company that would break everything if it stopped scaling with you?

post2.text:
A recent piece in HousingWire covered Buffini & Company's decision to add three senior executives to scale their coaching operations. On the surface, it reads like a staffing update. Look closer and there is a real lesson for any company pushing through significant revenue growth.

The companies we work with at Autobahn Consultants know this pattern well. When you are scaling fast, leadership capacity is always the first thing that breaks. Not systems. Not sales. Leadership.

Buffini did not just hire for function. They hired to extend the reach of their leadership model. That distinction matters more than most growth-stage companies realize until it is too late.

If your senior team is stretched thin and growth is stalling, the problem is probably sitting in your org chart.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMicEFVX3lxTE80SmJLNXdFS1VDcE9QNXQ2YzNFUDNUSzItcWljNWEyb2NEUVJ0UzdweEIzaVZyclAyWk5fcW54Nlo2U21GSVVoMHFJaVpLSFdaMU1tMXRIWU8wMTJvc3RJNTM5VkhoaVFxZnZFM0VlWVU

post3.text:
When companies make big leadership hires to fuel growth, they are asking the right question. They are just not always asking it early enough.

Chapter 1 of Rock Your Business, titled Lead Like Lives Depend On It, was written by Katherine Slain and it opens with a premise that stops most executives cold. Leadership at scale is not about adding capacity. It is about operating with a level of responsibility and clarity that most leaders have never been formally asked to develop.

Katherine draws a sharp line between leaders who grow a company and leaders who are actually built to scale one. The gap between those two is where most $50M to $500M journeys either accelerate or stall.

The chapter does not just name the problem. It shows you what leading at that level actually looks like in practice.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M assume their biggest growth constraint is strategy, systems, or capital. It is almost never any of those things. It is leadership, specifically whether the people at the top are operating at the level the business now demands. Here is what separates the executives who scale from the ones who quietly become the ceiling.

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs - J.P. Morgan
articles.snippet: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs  J.P. Morgan

articles.title: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now - inc.com
articles.snippet: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now  inc.com

post1.text:
Most business owners think about their exit too late. The real mistake is thinking about it at all like an exit.

Your valuation multiple is not set the quarter you decide to sell. It is built or destroyed over years of operational decisions, leadership depth, revenue concentration, and margin structure. By the time you are in a data room, the story is already written.

Inc. recently highlighted how an entire generation of business owners is approaching transition without a plan, and the financial consequences are significant. That pattern holds at every revenue level.

Here is what mid-market leaders need to understand:

One: Customer concentration above 20% will compress your multiple regardless of growth rate.

Two: Acquirers buy the business without you. If your name is on every major relationship, that is a liability, not an asset.

Three: EBITDA margin matters less than EBITDA quality. Recurring, predictable revenue commands a premium.

Your business is either building toward a strong multiple or drifting away from one right now. Which is it?

post2.text:
A recent piece from J.P. Morgan walks through exit strategy options for founders and entrepreneurs, covering everything from acquisitions to IPOs and management buyouts. It is a solid primer.

But here is the gap: most of that guidance is built around early-stage companies. The calculus shifts dramatically when you are running a $50M to $500M business.

At that scale, your exit is not a single event you plan for someday. It is the byproduct of how you run the business today. Multiple expansion, clean financials, leadership bench strength, and customer diversification are not exit prep tasks. They are operating disciplines.

At Autobahn Consultants, we work with leaders who want to scale with intention and capture full value when the time comes. If you are in that range and want to think more clearly about where your business stands, start here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixgFBVV95cUxPTU5NZlBZTkQwd29TWDREd3dhcVZjWHo4MS1JSVpOVU1UNHUtR29HNzdzR1BjS0NFbGl5V1l2UFhPMllFc1JjLVpyZEVzalhaS1VWZDdvQ1kwZGFkdDBfczlmb3o5bjU3NzR6SnhFOWdfM1ZYUkZuVEo4Qk9Ta3B6TnNJYUJ0OEVsMmZFWnVVSDlzMGZueWhGWXlSblQzYW1FOXFndkJ1Y00wODdLa296RlJ6UVhwVzQteHBpbnZCeG53Y2tSSmc

post3.text:
The U.S. Chamber of Commerce recently published a guide on how to build an exit strategy. The advice is reasonable. But for leaders running businesses in the $50M to $500M range, reasonable is not enough.

Chapter 2 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit. Authors Paul Belair and George Hohman go beyond the standard exit planning checklist and into the specific levers that drive valuation in the mid-market.

One thing they make clear: the actions that increase your multiple are not exit tasks. They are growth tasks. Waiting until you are ready to sell to address them is one of the most expensive mistakes a business owner can make.

If you are building a company worth acquiring, this chapter will change how you think about every major decision you make between now and that day.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most mid-market business owners think exit planning is something you do when you are ready to leave. That belief costs them millions. Your valuation multiple is not negotiated at the closing table. It is earned or eroded through years of operational choices. Customer concentration, leadership dependency, revenue quality, and margin structure are all being evaluated long before any buyer shows up. Here is what leaders scaling from $50M to $500M need to know now.
posts.email_subject: Your multiple is set before you decide to sell

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation  Business Wire

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

articles.title: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It - Forbes
articles.snippet: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It  Forbes

post1.text:
Most companies don't fail at strategy. They fail at the moment strategy meets reality.

Forbes recently reported that leadership teams consistently break down not during planning sessions, but during execution. The plan looks great on paper. The wheels come off when it hits the organization.

Here is what we see at this stage with $50M+ companies:

1. Strategy lives in the boardroom but never gets translated into weekly decisions at the manager level. If your team cannot connect their Tuesday priorities to your three-year goal, your strategy is a document, not a direction.

2. Accountability is assumed, not designed. Who owns what, by when, measured how? Vague answers here are where growth stalls.

3. Leadership teams confuse activity with progress. Busy is not the same as aligned.

Scaling from $50M to $500M does not require a better strategy half as often as it requires a more honest look at how execution actually works inside your company.

Where does your strategy break down first?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes breaks down exactly why leadership teams fail at strategy execution, and the findings are uncomfortable for anyone running a growing company.

The core problem is not a lack of smart plans. It is the gap between what gets decided in the conference room and what actually happens across the organization week to week.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. Growth exposes every crack in your execution infrastructure. The leaders who scale successfully are the ones who treat execution as a discipline, not an afterthought.

If your team is great at setting direction but struggles to sustain momentum, this article is worth your time.

Read it here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxOSXpNczJQdVBKTWlEV0dsby1FaEFGUVg5X1B3Zk9qUGI2bDJMSUxydGhRdURFUUdERXhBRmNzUndTVXhITTREMzMxdk9faE5rUldabk9ld2p2dHdfeXJjVEpMckJ6dFZNckFkTkk5ZmJ5MHRVSm5iSXo2WGlwc0NoeTBvcUYxTF9pbUNmaDB4LUdlNk9pMll0Y01BRTNVYzJObzRrV3pCN19scHYtdThYcHJVUVFYbjFMbkE

post3.text:
The execution gap is one of the most expensive problems a growing company can ignore.

In Chapter 3 of Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," authors Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall share what they discovered building and scaling businesses across completely different industries. The lesson that connects both? The companies that grew did not have more sophisticated strategies. They had better systems for making strategy stick at every level of the organization.

Rafa and Jim unpack a specific mindset shift that changes how leadership teams approach planning, and it is the kind of insight that sounds obvious in hindsight but is rarely practiced in practice.

If your strategy keeps stalling out between the planning session and the results, this chapter was written for you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams are not bad at strategy. They are bad at the 90 days after the strategy meeting. For companies scaling through the $50M to $500M range, the execution gap is where growth goes to die. The good news: it is a solvable problem. But only if you stop treating execution like a natural extension of planning and start treating it like a discipline of its own.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: 7 Key Drivers of B2B E-Commerce Growth - Small Business Trends
articles.snippet: 7 Key Drivers of B2B E-Commerce Growth  Small Business Trends

post1.text:
Most companies at $50M don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. A habit is what your team does by default. A strategy is a deliberate system built to scale. And right now, that gap is getting more expensive.

Gartner recently flagged something that should stop every sales leader cold: AI is saving reps time, but that saved time is not converting into revenue. Why? Because time savings without strategic direction just means your team is doing the wrong things faster.

Here's what scaling companies need to hear:

1. Efficiency tools only work inside a clear sales motion. Without one, you're optimizing chaos.

2. If your sales results depend on who shows up that day, you don't have a strategy. You have personalities.

3. Revenue predictability at $100M and beyond requires a documented, repeatable process that doesn't live inside anyone's head.

The question isn't whether you need a real sales strategy. The question is how many millions you've already left on the table by not having one.

post2.text:
A recent piece in Gartner caught our attention this week. The finding is blunt: AI is saving sales teams significant time, but CSOs are failing to redirect that time toward revenue-generating activity. The efficiency gain is real. The revenue gain is not.

For companies scaling past $50M, this is a critical warning. Adding technology to a broken or undefined sales process does not fix the process. It accelerates the dysfunction.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly. Leaders invest in tools before they invest in strategy. The result is a faster, more expensive version of what wasn't working before.

If your team has more tools than direction, that is where to start. Read the Gartner piece and ask yourself whether your CSO is steering the ship or just upgrading the engine.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg?oc=5

post3.text:
Gartner's latest research confirms what Deb and Brandon write about directly in Chapter 4 of Rock Your Business.

The chapter is called "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation." That title is not hyperbole. It is math.

As AI tools flood the sales world, the companies winning are not the ones with the best technology. They are the ones who built a real strategy first and layered tools on top of it. Deb and Brandon outline exactly what a scalable sales strategy looks like for companies in the $50M to $500M range, and why most organizations at that stage are still operating without one.

One thing they make clear: if your sales results cannot survive a personnel change, you do not have a strategy yet.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your sales team is busy. They are using new tools, logging activity, and hitting their calendars hard. But if revenue is still unpredictable, the problem is not effort. It is the absence of a real sales strategy. For companies between $50M and $500M, this is the most expensive gap in the business, and most leaders don't see it until growth stalls.
posts.email_subject: Your sales team is busy. That's the problem.

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

articles.title: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez - PRWeek
articles.snippet: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez  PRWeek

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is the feeling people get when they interact with your company, and most $50M+ businesses have no idea what that feeling actually is.

JBL recently made a deliberate shift away from campaign-driven marketing toward building around culture. The result is a brand that people belong to rather than just buy from. That distinction matters enormously at scale.

Here is what growing companies get wrong about branding:

1. They treat marketing as a department instead of a company-wide behavior. Every employee interaction is a brand moment.

2. They optimize for reach before they have established resonance. A bigger audience for a confused message accelerates the wrong outcome.

3. They measure clicks and impressions while ignoring perception. If you have never asked a customer what your brand makes them feel, you are flying blind.

Branding at scale is an inside job before it is an outside one. Culture shapes the message before the message ever reaches the market.

What does your brand actually feel like to someone who just became a customer?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators on JBL's marketing approach caught our attention. JBL is deliberately moving away from one-off campaigns and building its entire marketing engine around cultural identity instead.

For companies scaling past $50M, this is not just a creative choice. It is a structural one. Campaign-based marketing creates stop-and-start momentum. Culture-based marketing compounds over time because it gives your audience something to be part of, not just respond to.

The companies we work with that grow fastest are the ones whose internal culture and external brand are telling the same story. When those two things are misaligned, no budget fixes it.

If you are leading a growing company and your marketing feels like a series of disconnected pushes, it may be time to ask a harder question about what your brand actually stands for.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw?oc=5

post3.text:
JBL's decision to anchor its marketing in culture rather than campaigns is exactly the kind of strategic thinking that Chapter 5 of Rock Your Business explores in depth.

The chapter is called "What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?" and it is written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West. The title is intentional. Smell is the sense most tied to memory and emotion, and that is precisely the point. Your marketing leaves an impression whether you designed it to or not.

Kayli and Yasmin lay out a framework for understanding whether your brand is creating the right impression at scale, not just with customers, but internally with the people responsible for delivering on your brand promise every single day.

Most companies at $50M have a marketing function. Far fewer have a marketing identity. The gap between those two things is where growth stalls.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most growing companies treat branding as something marketing owns and everyone else ignores. That is exactly why so many $50M businesses plateau. Your brand is not a campaign or a color palette. It is the cumulative impression your company leaves on every person it touches. Get that wrong at scale, and more budget just amplifies the problem.
posts.email_subject: What does your brand actually smell like?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer - Pest Control Technology
articles.snippet: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer  Pest Control Technology

articles.title: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner  Business Wire

post1.text:
Hiring more people is not a growth strategy. It is a cost problem disguised as ambition.

SHRM recently reported that the most effective companies in 2026 are moving away from volume hiring and toward precision hiring. They are not filling seats. They are making deliberate bets on fewer, better-matched people.

Here is what that means for leaders scaling past $50M:

1. Your hiring process should define what a high performer actually looks like in each role before you post a single job. Most companies skip this step entirely.

2. Speed-to-hire is a vanity metric. Quality-of-hire measured at 90 and 180 days tells you whether your talent engine is working.

3. Every mis-hire at the senior level costs a scaling company far more than the salary. It costs momentum, team trust, and months you cannot recover.

Talent strategy is not an HR function. It is a CEO-level decision that compounds in both directions.

So here is the question worth sitting with: Do you actually know what a great hire looks like in your organization, or are you just hoping you will recognize one?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM lays out how the hiring playbook has fundamentally shifted. The companies winning in 2026 are not casting wide nets. They are getting precise about who they need, why they need them, and what success looks like before anyone is ever interviewed.

For companies in the $50M to $500M range, this distinction is not academic. It is the difference between building a team that scales and building one that stalls.

At Autobahn, we see it constantly. Leadership teams that treat hiring as a reactive process end up spending the next 18 months managing the fallout. The ones who build a deliberate talent engine before they feel the pressure? They scale faster and with far less friction.

Precision in hiring starts with clarity about your business model and where it is going. Read the full SHRM piece here and think about whether your process is built for where you are headed.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
The shift toward precision hiring that SHRM is reporting on is not new to the leaders we work with. It is something our consultants have been helping scaling companies build for years.

In Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business, "The Talent Engine," co-authors Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West break down exactly why talent strategy is the make-or-break factor for companies pushing from $50M toward $500M and beyond.

One thing Caleb, Darla, and Yasmin make clear early in the chapter: most scaling companies do not have a hiring problem. They have a clarity problem. And until leadership gets specific about what the business actually needs from each role, every hire is a gamble.

There is a framework in this chapter that changes how leadership teams think about building their bench. We will be sharing more soon.

Rock Your Business, by Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M think their talent problems are about pipeline. They are not. They are about precision. The businesses that successfully reach $500M and beyond are not out-hiring their competition. They are out-thinking them, building deliberate talent engines before the pressure hits. Here is what that actually requires.
posts.email_subject: Your hiring process is costing you scale

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 11 Key Hospitality KPIs to Track - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 11 Key Hospitality KPIs to Track  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
More metrics do not mean better decisions. For most companies between $50M and $500M, they mean slower ones.

A recent analysis from CLA CliftonLarsonAllen on manufacturer performance found something counterintuitive: companies tracking fewer, better-chosen KPIs consistently outperform those drowning in dashboards. The problem is not measurement. The problem is the belief that measuring more things creates more clarity.

Here is what we see constantly with scaling companies:

First, KPIs multiply faster than headcount. Every new leader inherits a metric and adds three more. Nobody retires the old ones.

Second, teams start managing the numbers instead of the business. When a metric becomes a target, it stops being useful information.

Third, the KPIs that mattered at $50M are often the wrong ones at $150M. Growth changes what you should be watching, and most leadership teams never revisit that question.

The discipline is not in tracking. It is in cutting.

What would happen if your leadership team eliminated half your current KPIs tomorrow?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Business.com walked through 14 tools companies can use to track KPIs more effectively. Solid list. But here is what the article does not address: the tool is rarely the problem.

For companies scaling past $50M, the real issue is alignment, not software. We work with leadership teams who have every dashboard imaginable and still cannot agree in a Monday meeting on whether last quarter was good or not. Why? Because different departments are measuring different things, with no shared definition of what winning looks like.

Before you evaluate another tracking tool, get your leadership team to answer one question: which five numbers, if every person in this company understood them, would change how we operate?

Start there. The right tool follows the right question.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMingFBVV95cUxQcWNPSTVTUDVYYURoVkZKa0ItNEtQbmJqWlNJTlIzUVZqclAxMHFaajNobC1JbGNkZGZVajN6MWJLT1pJcXktVG10VG8zUUljdU43NU5WR1cyNDB4QkxONlJkb3hJR2FfNzdGZHd1VWhPbTktdU1vdTEzamFWY2ZFVDNKUFIwRG91ZDdWU1UyN2twNFpLd1JjU3EzTUpjdw?oc=5

post3.text:
CLA CliftonLarsonAllen recently published guidance on which KPIs manufacturers should prioritize to improve performance. It is a useful framework. But chapter 7 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, goes one level deeper.

The chapter is called Death by KPIs, written by Hector Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall. Their core argument is one most growing companies are not ready to hear: the KPIs killing your business are not the wrong ones. They are the right ones from an earlier version of your company that you never let go of.

Scaling from $50M toward $500M requires a deliberate audit of what you measure and why. Not once. Repeatedly. The authors lay out a clear-eyed process for doing exactly that without gutting accountability in the process.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams at $50M and beyond are not suffering from a lack of data. They are suffering from a surplus of the wrong data, measured for the wrong stage of growth. The metrics that helped you build the business can quietly become the ones holding it back. Here is how to tell the difference and what to do about it.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs scaling with you?

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

articles.title: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago - Fortune
articles.snippet: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago  Fortune

post1.text:
Most companies are failing at AI not because they chose the wrong tools, but because they never changed how they measure work.

Fortune recently reported that thousands of CEOs saw no meaningful impact on productivity or employment from AI adoption. Economists are dusting off a 40-year-old paradox to explain it. The pattern is familiar: powerful technology arrives, and organizations try to bolt it onto old workflows without rethinking the fundamentals.

Here is what leaders scaling past $50M need to understand:

First, AI does not fix broken processes. It accelerates them, good or bad. Second, if your performance metrics still measure inputs rather than outcomes, your team has no incentive to use AI in ways that actually move the business. Third, the executives seeing real gains are redesigning accountability structures alongside the technology, not after it.

Adopting AI without updating how you measure performance is like installing a faster engine in a car with no steering wheel.

What does your current performance framework actually reward?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times explored how busy executives are now deploying AI digital twins to handle meetings, answer messages, and represent them in conversations they cannot attend.

This is not a novelty. This is a signal.

For companies in the $50M to $500M growth corridor, time is the most constrained resource at the top. When a CEO is the bottleneck, the whole organization slows down. AI tools that extend executive bandwidth are not about convenience. They are about removing a structural growth constraint.

The leaders who figure out how to delegate intelligently to both people and technology will scale faster than those waiting for the tools to mature.

If you are leading a scaling company, ask yourself where you are personally the ceiling. That is where AI belongs first.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ

post3.text:
Harvard Business Review is arguing that AI demands entirely new performance metrics. We have been saying something similar to our clients for a while now.

Chapter 8 of our upcoming book Rock Your Business, titled The Third Monitor Theory, tackles exactly this territory. Co-authored by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain, the chapter explores how scaling companies need to rethink the way leaders integrate AI into daily decision-making, not just daily tasks.

The insight that will stay with you: most executives already have the information they need to make better decisions. The real question is whether they have built the right structure around that information to act on it faster than their competition.

That is what the Third Monitor Theory is about. And no, it is not literally about a computer monitor.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Thousands of CEOs adopted AI and got nothing measurable in return. That is not a technology problem. It is a strategy problem. Companies scaling from $50M to $500M cannot afford to treat AI as an experiment with no accountability attached. The rules of adoption have changed, and the leaders who understand that are already pulling ahead.
posts.email_subject: Why most CEOs got nothing from AI

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

post1.text:
The leaders most likely to sabotage their company's growth are the ones who've already earned the right to be in the room.

Imposter syndrome doesn't disappear when you hit $50M in revenue. According to research covered in Forbes Australia, high achievers carry self-doubt further up the ladder, not less. The bigger the stakes, the louder the internal critic.

Here's what that means for scaling companies:

1. Leaders who privately doubt themselves often overcorrect by micromanaging, slowing down the very decisions that drive growth.

2. The same self-scrutiny that built the business can become a ceiling once the org chart grows beyond what one person can see.

3. Naming the pattern inside your leadership team is not a soft conversation. It is a strategic one. Companies that scale past $100M consistently have leaders who can separate their identity from their performance metrics.

You got to $50M by being the smartest person in the room. What happens when the room gets bigger than you?

#Leadership #ScalingBusiness #ExecutiveMindset

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company makes a provocative argument: we should stop calling it imposter syndrome entirely.

The framing matters. When leaders at scaling companies treat self-doubt as a personal defect, they try to fix themselves in private. When they treat it as a normal byproduct of operating in genuinely new territory, they build systems and teams around it instead.

That distinction is critical between $50M and $500M. Growth at that level requires leaders to consistently step into roles they have never held before. Doubt is not a warning sign. Ignoring it is.

The companies we work with that scale fastest are not run by fearless leaders. They are run by leaders who act despite the fear and build honest cultures where others can too.

Read the full article and think about how your leadership team talks about uncertainty internally.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidkFVX3lxTE9PRnBrU1NWWm1BeEl3dk5qVjFRc0M0T2ctZW9fbjdWd2NmdFlUZVVNeW9JV0VTWkktVmp0MlVjbFVEMXEweWZBS1d6bFBSMVNoNFVfWm5WaFo0aG1PT1RJN0p2Ylc0SkRONnBBcDYzZmNEUUJsRFE

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and it tackles something most business books skip entirely: the psychological weight that comes with scaling.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, this chapter speaks directly to leaders who have built something real and still find themselves wondering if they belong at the next level.

One thing the chapter makes clear: imposter syndrome at the $50M to $500M stage is not about confidence. It is about the gap between the leader you were and the leader the business now requires. Closing that gap takes more than mindset work. It takes a deliberate approach to how you lead, delegate, and grow alongside the company.

There is a framework inside this chapter that reframes the question entirely. We will let the book do the talking.

Rock Your Business, by Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader who has scaled past $50M knows the feeling: the revenue is real, the team is growing, and somehow the doubt is louder than ever. Imposter syndrome does not shrink as companies scale. It evolves. And for executives navigating the road to $500M, ignoring it is not a coping strategy. It is a liability. Here is what the research and hard-won experience actually say.
posts.email_subject: The doubt that scales with you

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
The goal should not be work-life balance. It should be a life you actually designed.

Most leaders scaling past $50M are so focused on the business that they treat their personal life as whatever is left over. That is not a strategy. That is a slow drift toward regret.

Morgan Stanley recently published research showing that entrepreneurs who sell their businesses often struggle deeply with identity and purpose afterward, not because they failed, but because they never defined what they were building toward beyond the exit.

Three things worth sitting with if you lead a $50M+ company:

1. Your business plan is detailed. Your life plan probably does not exist.
2. The habits that got you to $50M will not automatically make you a fulfilled person at $500M or after an exit.
3. Time is the one resource you cannot scale or recover.

The leaders who navigate growth without losing themselves are not lucky. They are intentional.

What would your life look like if you applied the same rigor to designing it as you do to running your business?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, citing research from Morgan Stanley, laid out a clear roadmap for entrepreneurs navigating life after a business exit. The findings are worth paying attention to well before you reach that point.

Here is what we see consistently at Autobahn: founders and CEOs scaling from $50M toward $500M treat life planning as something to figure out later. Later never arrives on schedule.

The companies that scale well tend to be led by people who have clarity not just on their business goals but on what they want their life to actually look like. That clarity sharpens decisions, reduces burnout, and keeps the right people in the room longer.

If you are in growth mode right now, this article is worth ten minutes of your time.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
Morgan Stanley's research on life after a business exit raises a question most scaling leaders avoid: who are you building this for, and what happens to you when you get there?

Chapter 10 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, takes this head-on. The chapter is called Coming Up for Air, and it was written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain.

One of the core ideas in that chapter is this: life planning is not a reward for reaching the finish line. It is infrastructure for the journey. Leaders who treat it that way make better decisions, sustain their energy longer, and build companies that do not depend entirely on their sacrifice.

There is a framework inside Chapter 10 that changes how growth-stage leaders think about their time, their identity, and what they are actually optimizing for.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most $50M business leaders have a detailed growth strategy and no life plan. That gap does not stay quiet forever. It shows up in burnout, bad decisions, and exits that feel emptier than expected. The leaders scaling toward $500M who actually thrive are the ones who design their life with the same seriousness they bring to their business. Here is what that looks like in practice.
posts.email_subject: Your business plan exists. Does your life plan?
